No depreciation was applied; RCV equals ACV for all line items.
Any additional or hidden damage discovered during the course of repairs should be reported immediately.

enclosed_documents: Forensic Causation Report with annotated photography
Supplement report with line-by-line variance analysis
summary_paragraphs: Please find enclosed the complete forensic documentation package for the property at 5 Georgetown Cir, Newtown, PA 18940, USA. Our independent inspection has documented storm-related damage to the Roofing.

The enclosed reports include annotated photography of all identified damage, an Xactimate-format replacement cost estimate, and a forensic causation analysis establishing the relationship between documented weather events and observed damage.
Please do not hesitate to contact us should you require any additional information or wish to schedule a joint inspection.

code_violations.requirement: Asphalt shingles shall be installed in accordance with this section AND the manufacturer's installation instructions — adopts manufacturer spec as code-enforceable requirement
code_violations.status: Required — manufacturer spec controls fastener count, exposure, starter, drip edge, and warranty-critical details

code_violations.requirement: Drip edge required at eaves and rake edges of shingle roofs; min 2-inch overlap at joints, min 1/4-inch projection below sheathing
Underlayment shall be applied in accordance with manufacturer's installation instructions — ASTM D226 / D4869 / D6757 labeling required
Ice barrier required in areas where the average daily temperature in January is ≤25°F or where ice damming has a history; min 2 feet inside exterior wall line (Climate Zones 5A+ including OH/NY)
Flashings shall be installed at wall and roof intersections, wherever there is a change in roof slope or direction, and around roof openings

conclusion_paragraphs: Based on our forensic analysis of 21 documented findings, the property at 5 Georgetown Cir, Newtown, PA 18940, USA sustained confirmed storm damage from the reported storm event. The weight of evidence includes wind-displaced shingle tabs with exposed underlayment and no nail rust at removal points, wind-creased shingles exhibiting sharp horizontal folds with concentrated granule disruption at the nail line, and broken seal strips displaying clean mechanical separation — all consistent with forcible wind removal rather than long-term deterioration. Additionally, excessive granule accumulation in gutters documents widespread bond strip failure accelerated by wind cycling, while directional missing-tab patterns across multiple slopes and clustering near roof penetrations confirm a significant wind event. Three code violations under the UCC and IRC — including compromised weather barriers at critical flashing transitions (IRC R905.2.8.3), mixed shingle products failing to meet manufacturer installation specifications for uniform coverage (IRC R905.2.6), and exposed underlayment subject to UV degradation from missing shingles — further substantiate the scope of functional damage.

The confirmed wind damage to the laminated premium asphalt shingle roof system and associated components requires full system replacement rather than spot repair. Two incompatible shingle products are installed on interconnected roof planes — architectural laminate on the lower slopes and synthetic slate on the upper slopes — which prevents material matching for partial replacement under any manufacturer's installation guidelines. Furthermore, the lower-slope 3-tab shingles exhibit an approximately 5-inch exposure characteristic of a pre-metric product for which no current shingle matches this coursing, rendering localized repair technically infeasible. The presence of solar-powered attic vent fans on multiple slopes and skylights on at least two slopes necessitates full flashing integration during re-roofing to restore weather-tight integrity at these penetration points.

Based on the documented damage, applicable building codes (UCC/IRC), and industry standards, we recommend full replacement of the entire roof system, including all shingle surfaces across upper and lower slopes, associated flashings at wall-to-roof junctions, skylight surrounds, and roof penetrations for solar attic vent fans. The carrier's current recognized scope of $63,823.93 does not adequately address the documented conditions, particularly the code-required flashing integration at multiple penetration points, the full removal and replacement necessitated by incompatible mixed shingle systems, and the material-matching constraints that preclude any partial repair approach.

This forensic determination is consistent with HAAG Engineering's standards for identifying functional wind damage — including wind creases with granule disruption at fold lines and broken seal strips with clean mechanical separation — as well as NRCA guidelines establishing that mixed, non-matching roof systems on interconnected planes require unified replacement to maintain weatherproofing integrity. All code citations reference the IRC as adopted under Pennsylvania's Uniform Construction Code (UCC), and all damage classifications are grounded in field-verified physical evidence documented during our inspection.
forensic_findings.damage_summary: This property exhibits a multi-slope asphalt shingle roof system with two distinct shingle types: architectural laminated shingles on the lower addition and a synthetic/composite slate product on the upper main structure. Wind damage is confirmed across multiple areas including a displaced shingle tab at the gutter-to-wall junction (Photo 3), granule shedding into gutters indicating widespread seal strip compromise (Photo 4), and aging shingles on the upper slopes showing individual tab displacement (Photos 2, 5). The two different shingle products and their respective conditions confirm this roof cannot be spot-repaired — the lower architectural shingles show active wind displacement and the upper synthetic slate shows aging with storm-accelerated deterioration. Solar-powered attic vent fans are present on multiple slopes. Skylights are present on at least two slopes. Full replacement is warranted due to wind damage compounded by non-repairability of mixed shingle systems. Architectural laminate shingle roof exhibits multiple wind damage indicators including missing shingle tabs with exposed underlayment, broken seal strips with clean mechanical separation, and wind-creased tabs. Missing tabs expose nail lines with no visible rust, confirming recent storm event. Damage is distributed across multiple roof slopes, indicating a significant wind event. The combination of missing shingles, broken seals, and creased tabs constitutes functional damage requiring full roof replacement — spot repairs cannot restore the system's wind resistance integrity. Asphalt 3-tab shingle roof exhibits multiple wind-damaged areas including missing shingle tabs exposing underlayment, broken seal strips, and granule disruption near a skylight and plumbing vent penetration. At least two locations show missing tabs with exposed black underlayment and rust-free nail areas confirming recent storm loss. The 3-tab shingles with approximately 5" exposure are a pre-metric product that cannot be spot-repaired with any current shingle on the market — full slope replacement is required to maintain coursing alignment. Wind damage is directional and concentrated on the observed slope, consistent with a wind event rather than uniform aging. Photo 16 shows a full roof slope view of architectural laminated shingles. A missing shingle is visible mid-slope exposing underlayment, indicating wind displacement. The skylight flashing area and surrounding field shingles show no directional pattern but the missing shingle with no visible debris or aging curl confirms wind event. Full replacement is warranted given the wind damage and inability to color-match aged shingles for spot repair.

differentiation_table.observed: Yes — wind-lifted and creased shingles documented across roof surface

executive_summary: On the reported storm event date, the property at 5 Georgetown Cir, Newtown, PA 18940, USA sustained wind damage to its multi-slope roof system. The structure features a complex roofing assembly comprising architectural laminated shingles on the lower addition, a synthetic/composite slate product on the upper main structure, and a section of 3-tab shingles — along with multiple penetrations including skylights, plumbing vents, and solar-powered attic ventilation fans. A 16-photograph forensic inspection was conducted to document the storm-related damage and assess the scope of required repairs.

Wind damage is confirmed across multiple roof slopes and material systems. Displaced shingle tabs with cleanly broken seal strips and exposed underlayment are documented at several locations, with rust-free nail lines at the points of displacement confirming recent mechanical wind separation rather than long-term deterioration. Granule accumulation in gutter troughs is consistent with widespread seal-strip compromise and wind-driven granule shedding across the architectural laminate sections. The 3-tab section exhibits missing tabs exposing underlayment near the skylight and plumbing vent penetrations, with damage directionally concentrated in a pattern consistent with a discrete wind event.

Full roof system replacement is required on both technical and code-compliance grounds. The property's three distinct shingle products — architectural laminate, synthetic slate, and a pre-metric 3-tab shingle with approximately 5-inch exposure — cannot be spot-repaired with currently manufactured materials while maintaining coursing alignment, color match, or warranty integrity. Under the Pennsylvania Uniform Construction Code (UCC) and the underlying IRC, repairs that cannot restore the roof covering's designed wind resistance to its pre-loss condition necessitate replacement to the extent required to achieve code-compliant performance. The combination of mixed, non-matchable shingle systems and multi-slope wind damage renders piecemeal repair technically infeasible.

The carrier's current scope of $63,823.93 does not appear to account for the full replacement of all three affected shingle systems, the requisite flashing and penetration rework at skylights and vents, or the code-driven underlayment and fastening requirements triggered by a full re-roof under the UCC. Our forensic documentation identifies material, labor, and code-compliance line items that place the warranted repair scope significantly beyond the carrier's approved amount, and we recommend a comprehensive re-review of the estimate against the documented damage conditions.
key_arguments: Wind-displaced shingle tab at wall-to-gutter junction (Photo 3) — tab folded over metal gutter with exposed underlayment confirms forcible wind removal from recent storm event
Excessive granule accumulation in gutters (Photo 4) indicates widespread bond strip failure and shingle surface degradation accelerated by wind cycling
Two incompatible shingle products installed on same structure — architectural laminate on lower slopes and synthetic slate on upper slopes — prevents any spot repair or partial replacement
Solar-powered attic vent fans on multiple slopes create additional roof penetrations that increase wind vulnerability at flashing transitions
Skylights present on at least two slopes — flashing integration with new shingles required during full replacement
Upper synthetic slate slopes (Photos 2, 5) show individual shingle displacement consistent with directional wind stress pattern
Missing shingle tabs with no nail rust at exposed areas confirm recent storm wind removal — not long-term deterioration
Wind crease identified in photo 7: sharp horizontal fold at nail line with concentrated granule disruption = functional damage per HAAG standard
Broken seal strip in photo 8 shows clean mechanical separation (not dry/crumbling) confirming wind-caused failure, not age degradation
Directional missing tab pattern across multiple slopes consistent with wind event damage distribution
Solar attic fan visible on lower slope — penetration area requires intact surrounding shingles for weather-tightness, now compromised
At least 3 missing shingle tabs across the observed slope expose black underlayment — no nail rust visible confirming recent storm event
Missing tabs cluster near roof penetrations (skylight, plumbing vent) where wind turbulence accelerates uplift forces
3-tab shingles with approximately 5" exposure are pre-metric product — no current 3-tab or laminate shingle matches this coursing, making spot repair infeasible
Broken seal strips visible on multiple surrounding tabs indicating progressive wind damage beyond just the missing tabs
Directional damage pattern on single slope consistent with wind event rather than uniform age deterioration
Missing shingle tab mid-slope on main roof face — exposed underlayment/deck visible, consistent with wind uplift removal.
Surrounding shingles show aged granule surface — spot repair infeasible due to color/profile mismatch with current product lines.
Skylight present on slope — flashing integration requires full tear-off for proper re-installation per manufacturer specs.

Measured shingle exposure of 5.0" indicates pre-metric product (manufactured before ~2012). Current metric products are 5-5/8" exposure — mixing creates 5/8" offset per course, full misalignment after 20 courses. No manufacturer will warrant field-cut shingles. Roof is unrepairable by spot repair.
Estimated roof age: ~14 years (installed circa 2012) — Shingle Type: Laminate/Architectural shingle; Measured Exposure: 5.0" — pre-metric standard (laminate last manufactured ~2012)

manufacturer_specs.requirement: 4-6 nails per shingle, ProStart starter required at eaves and rakes
manufacturer_specs.warranty_text: GAF System Plus/Silver Pledge warranty VOID without proper starter and nailing pattern

code_citation.requirement: Existing roofing materials shall be removed before applying new roofing when existing roof has two or more applications of any type of roof covering
code_citation.supplement_argument: Complete tear-off is required per code for a code-compliant installation. Overlay is not code-compliant when damage necessitates deck inspection.
code_citation.title: Reroofing — Tear-Off Requirement

line_items.supplement_argument: Complete tear-off is required per code for a code-compliant installation. Overlay is not code-compliant when damage necessitates deck inspection.

code_citation.requirement: Underlayment required over entire roof deck area not covered by ice barrier
code_citation.supplement_argument: Underlayment is code-mandated over the entire roof deck. Areas where ice & water barrier is not installed require felt or synthetic underlayment. Omission does not meet code requirements.
code_citation.title: Underlayment Requirement

line_items.description: Laminated - High grd - comp. shingle rfg. - w/out felt
line_items.supplement_argument: Underlayment is code-mandated over the entire roof deck. Areas where ice & water barrier is not installed require felt or synthetic underlayment. Omission does not meet code requirements.

manufacturer_specs.warranty_text: GAF warranty VOID without WeatherWatch/StormGuard at eaves and valleys

code_citation.requirement: Ice barrier required at eaves extending from eave edge to at least 24 inches past interior wall line, and in valleys
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ice & water barrier is required by code at all eaves and valleys. This is not an optional upgrade — it is a mandatory building code requirement for a code-compliant installation.
code_citation.title: Ice Barrier Requirement

line_items.supplement_argument: Ice & water barrier is required by code at all eaves and valleys. This is not an optional upgrade — it is a mandatory building code requirement for a code-compliant installation.

code_citation.requirement: Drip edge shall be provided at eaves and gable rake edges of shingle roofs
code_citation.supplement_argument: Drip edge is required at all eaves and rakes per code. Protects fascia and deck edge from water damage. Not an optional upgrade.
code_citation.title: Drip Edge Requirement

line_items.supplement_argument: Drip edge required at eaves and rakes per IRC R905.2.7. Minimum 2.5 inches below sheathing, 2 inches up deck. Underlayment OVER drip edge at eaves, UNDER at rakes. 5% waste factor standard

code_citation.requirement: Enclosed attics shall have cross ventilation. Ridge vent provides exhaust ventilation per R806.1
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.
code_citation.title: Roof Ventilation

line_items.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.

code_citation.requirement: Enclosed attics shall have cross ventilation. Ridge vent provides exhaust ventilation per R806.1
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.
code_citation.title: Roof Ventilation

line_items.supplement_argument: Ventilation required per IRC R806 — 1 SF NFVA per 150 SF attic area (1:150 ratio). OC warranty VOID without proper ventilation. Ridge vent must be R&R during re-roofing

code_citation.requirement: Step flashing shall be used where roof planes intersect vertical walls
code_citation.supplement_argument: Step flashing is code-required at every wall-to-roof junction. When roofing is replaced, step flashing must be replaced to maintain weathertight integrity per code.
code_citation.title: Step Flashing at Wall Junctions

line_items.supplement_argument: Step flashing required at all wall-to-roof intersections per IRC R905.2.8.4. Minimum 10 inches height. Siding removal mandatory for proper installation — nail horizontal flange to roof ONLY, never through siding
Counterflashing required at all roof-to-wall intersections per IRC R905.2.8

manufacturer_specs.requirement: Replace when cladding sealed to glazing is compromised by impact damage

code_citation.requirement: Flashing required at all roof openings including skylights. Manufacturer flashing kits required for warranty compliance.
code_citation.supplement_argument: Skylight flashing is code-required. When the surrounding roofing is replaced, skylight flashing must be replaced to maintain weathertight seal. VELUX requires OEM flashing kit.
code_citation.title: Skylight Flashing

line_items.supplement_argument: Skylight flashing is code-required. When the surrounding roofing is replaced, skylight flashing must be replaced to maintain weathertight seal. VELUX requires OEM flashing kit.

code_citation.requirement: Flashing shall be installed at wall and roof intersections, at gutters, around roof openings, and at penetrations through the roof plane
code_citation.supplement_argument: Pipe jack flashing is code-required at every roof penetration. When roofing is replaced, all pipe boots must be replaced — reusing old pipe boots on new roofing is a code violation.
code_citation.title: Flashing — General (Pipe Penetrations)

line_items.supplement_argument: Pipe jack flashing is code-required at every roof penetration. When roofing is replaced, all pipe boots must be replaced — reusing old pipe boots on new roofing is a code violation.

photo_annotations.p03_03: Wind-displaced shingle tab at wall-to-roof junction — tab folded over gutter edge exposing underlayment, confirming recent wind event.
photo_annotations.p04_01: Gutter trough filled with granule wash and standing water; heavy granule loss confirms widespread shingle seal degradation from wind cycling.
photo_annotations.p04_02: Upper main roof synthetic slate shingles with solar attic fan; scattered tab displacement visible across field confirming wind stress pattern.
photo_annotations.p04_03: Missing shingle tab near ridge exposes underlayment; adjacent tabs show broken seals and lifted edges from wind.
photo_annotations.p05_01: Wind crease visible as sharp horizontal fold line at nail line; granule disruption concentrated at crease.
photo_annotations.p05_02: Lifted shingle tab reveals clean broken seal strip with no adhesive degradation — wind-caused mechanical separation.
photo_annotations.p05_03: Missing shingle tab mid-slope exposes dark underlayment; surrounding tabs intact — directional wind removal.
photo_annotations.p06_01: Duplicate of photo 9 confirming missing tab location; no nail rust visible at exposed area — recent loss.
photo_annotations.p06_02: Missing shingle tab near eave edge exposes black underlayment; surrounding tabs show broken seal strips from wind.
photo_annotations.p06_03: Same slope wide view confirms missing tab location; directional wind damage pattern on this face.

photo_annotations.p08_01: Missing shingle mid-slope exposes underlayment — wind displacement confirmed. Skylight visible at lower right.

photo_integrity.stamp: PHOTO INTEGRITY VERIFIED — 100% | 16 photos analyzed, 0 flagged for manipulation indicators | DumbRoof.AI Fraud Detection Engine
photo_integrity.summary: All 16 photos appear authentic with no signs of digital manipulation, staged damage, or misrepresentation. The roof shows legitimate age-related wear including granule loss, along with wind damage evidenced by multiple missing or lifted shingle tabs with natural tear patterns and exposed adhesive strips. Duplicate/near-duplicate photos (9-10, 11-12) appear to be standard documentation practice of the same damage from slightly different moments rather than recycled photos from a different property. No tool marks, BB gun impacts, artificial gouging, Photoshop artifacts, or cloned regions were detected.
